-- 1 of 5 --

Open Cart Considerations
!
I want to talk about your actual sequence of your open cart. This is more about
e-mails than how you’re messaging your list.
You want to leave your cart open anywhere from three to seven days. I think for
most people five to seven days is that golden period. If you go longer than seven days,
in my opinion, it ends up turning into a beg-a-thon because you're going to want to
keep up the momentum and keep up the pressure in continuing to e-mail throughout
your open cart. Even at seven days usually at the end of it it feels like you're running
on fumes, you been sending the same type of e-mails over and over so that's why I like
to do it five to seven days. You could do shorter and I think it's more of an advance
technique to go shorter. A lot of times I have gone a day and a half, two days, maybe
three days for my launches but I've done a lot of them.
The thing is with the shorter launches it gives you less time to adjust if things
are not going the way you want them to go. That sales video just really isn't all that
good, it's not converting as well as I want so I'm going to re-shoot the sales video.
Well if you've only got the cart open for two or three days it's hard to do something like
that. If you have it open five to seven days you can adjust. Some partners that didn't
get their e-mail out for some reason, that's fine, they can adjust. So shorter period
launches they really build scarcity and position in moving forward in the market but
things can go wrong. I think five to seven days is that golden period.
Then favor opening days are Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday. Friday also works.
I've actually had a lot of Friday opens and they've worked just fine. You generally don't
want to open on the weekends. Now this is market dependent; there are some market
where it's better to open on the weekends. I've heard the dating advice market works
really great on Saturday because all these guys are home on Saturdays wishing they
had a date. So different markets can react differently but across most markets,
Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, specifically Tuesday and Thursday are good days to
open up.
Favorite closing days in general for most markets you want to avoid the
weekends. If you are going to close on the weekend then don't do it on Saturday, close
on Sunday. Also you want to close at midnight. You don't want to close in the middle
of the day. Close at midnight otherwise you're leaving money on the table.
Here are some of the ways I have structured it and there's a lot of different
ways, lot of flexibility. Open Monday close Friday, that's a five day launch. I've done
that recently, it's worked out pretty well. Open Tuesday close Thursday, that's a short
one, a three day launch. I've opened Wednesday and closed Friday. That's a three day
launch as well. It's just moved one day there so you see how flexible you can be. I've
opened up on Tuesday and closed on the following Monday. That's a seven day launch
that bridged across a weekend. I've opened on Thursday and closed on Monday, that's
Copyright © All Rights Reserved Internet Alchemy, Inc. 2014 -2015 	Page ! 2
PLF_Module6v1.0
GROUPINSIDERS.COM EXCLUSIVE

-- 2 of 5 --

a five day launch that bridged a weekend. I've opened on Thursday and closed on
Wednesday, a seven day launch that bridged the weekend.
Obviously you can move these around. All these are five or seven or three. Can
you do six or four? Absolutely. You can open on Thursday and close on Tuesday, that's
a six day launch. The reality is most people are on mine almost every day. In general
e-mails get opened more on Tuesday and Wednesdays and Thursdays than they do on
Mondays or Saturdays. In general, in most markets. But you can test this out. If
you're just watching your e-mails and watching when the open rates tend to be, you'll
see what are the good days in your market. But this isn't rocket science and it isn't
science whatsoever, there is a lot of flexibility. Don't worry about which days, don't
worry about that, it's not important, just get the open right and you'll be fine.
Now, when do you send e-mails during a launch, or during your open cart? And
the answer is you generally send them a lot more than people think you send them. So
on your cart opening day, you want to send two e-mails. Actually I usually send three
e-mails. I send an e-mail before the cart opens giving people the final instructions,
telling them what to expect once the cart opens, and then you send the old, "We're
open, go buy it, click here, go check it out, you been looking forward to it." So that's a
very short e-mail - 15 words, "Hey we're open, click here." It can be very, very short e-
mail. Hopefully they're anticipating that.
Then maybe three, four, five hours after you open cart you want to send the
tsunami e-mail. This is something I've been teaching since the first version of PLF
probably around 2005. The tsunami e-mail is this e-mail where you're giving the
impression, and this is often the case, that you're just trying to keep your head above
water. That this whole thing has become this epic event that's carrying, threatening to
carry you away. The feeling in this e-mail is we're all in this together, hang in there, I'm
just trying to stay one step ahead of you and keep the server up, and make sure
everything runs well.
On opening cart day there's often some quirky thing happens that you don't
expect that throws a curve ball at you. Use that as a reason to e-mail people and say,
"Hey, you might have had some trouble getting through the site," or "We had this link
that wasn't working, thanks for hanging in there. The good news is we've got
everything fixed, and orders going through OK, lots of people have already jumped on
board." If it's a digital product, "Some people are already in the membership site and
they're building a great community." That's the second e-mail on opening day.
Then you want to e-mail every single day during your launch. So if you have a
seven day open cart, that means you send those two e-mails on day one. Then day
two, three, four, five, and six you're sending an additional e-mail every single day.
Often I'm sending an FAQ e-mail, I might send a little bonus video, but you just keep on
coming up with reasons to mail them. That's why I say, once you get that launch
longer than seven days, it's hard to keep on coming up with reasons to keep on mailing
them. You can send e-mails talking about some of the objections and answering some
of the objections. Talk about what's happening with the people that actually bought
Copyright © All Rights Reserved Internet Alchemy, Inc. 2014 -2015 	Page ! 3
PLF_Module6v1.0
GROUPINSIDERS.COM EXCLUSIVE

-- 3 of 5 --

your product, You've actually got it in their hands now, the results you're getting, or the
feedback you're getting from them.
Then the day before you close cart, you want to start talking about the scarcity.
When I say close cart, not everyone is going to close their shopping cart and take their
product off the market. Lot of people do with their launches but there always has to be
some definitive end to your launch. You had an open and then you have a close for the
launch. The product might stay on the market but something bad happens if they don't
buy. You create a deadline at the end of your launch.
Generally there's three ways to create scarcity. First is that bonuses go away so
the offer gets less attractive. You put together this bonus package for the launch and
the bonuses go away. The second is the price goes up. There's this [inaudible 08:30]
launch special, you can get it for $97, after the launch it's going to be $197. So that's
reason number two or scarcity reason number two. The third one is the project comes
off the market, you're actually going to close the cart and they aren't going to be able
to get it. Those three, I named them in that order, that's the order of power. From the
least powerful to the most powerful. The least powerful is the bonuses go away. Still
powerful, still good, scarcity, but that's less powerful than the price is going up. Price is
going up is strong but it's not as strong as the projects actually coming off the market.
Actually you can combine all three. You can say hey, "We're going to close the
cart and after we close the cart, we might in the future, bring this back on the market.
When we do, if we do, all these bonuses, Bonus A, B, and C that I told you about, we're
not going to offer those ever again. And when we bring it back the price is going to go
up to X. You got to act by tonight or else it's coming off the market and if we ever
release it again, you're not going to be able to get these bonuses, and the price will go
up to X. That's combining all three.
But any way, the day before you close cart you start messaging scarcity. If
you're going to close on a Friday, you open on Monday you send two e-mails on
Monday, you send another e-mail on Tuesday, one on Wednesday, one on Thursday.
You might even send two on Thursday. Send one early in the morning, that might be
an FAQ, or a little bit of content, or answering objection. But then on midday on
Thursday, you would send something that says hey, "We're going to be closing down
tomorrow. If you want to jump on board you got to act now, we're closing tomorrow at
midnight." And you do want to close at midnight. I think I mentioned that already.
You don't want to close in the middle of the day. So you close at midnight.
So the day before cart close, you want to start messaging scarcity, possibly send
two e-mails on that day. Then on closing day you want to send two or three e-mails.
Lot of people are like, "Jeff I can't send two e-mails. I've never sent two e-mails on the
same day before." The thing is you don't have to send two e-mails if you want. The
only thing is you're going to leave half your sales on the table. Because half of your
sales likely will come in on your last day. That's why it's important to mail multiple
times.

-- 4 of 5 --

Three e-mails, how do you actually send three e-mails to your list? Well at six
am. You send an e-mail says you're closing tonight at midnight. Midnight whatever
Pacific time, Eastern time, whatever you want, we're closing tonight at midnight. Then
in the middle of the day you send another e-mail that says we're going to close in 12
hours. Then at six pm. You send them, "This is your last chance." Often I'll send a
thank you.
This last one will be, "Hey I just wanted to . . . Wow this has been a crazy
experience, I've put out all these videos, got all these comments, it's just been a
wonderful experience to go through, thanks for sharing it with me. Thank you for
everyone whose already jumped on board with me. We've already got tons of people in
the membership site and they're all creating this great community together. Oh by the
way, if you haven't jumped on board you still have a few hours to go and we're closing
tonight at midnight." That's how you do three e-mails on the final day.
You definitely follow this. At times people pull a punch. You got to go for the
jugular, you have to close this thing down with power and this is exactly how you do it.
Send two e-mails on that first day, send an additional e-mail every day your open, the
second to last day start sending scarcity e-mail, consider sending two e-mails on that
day. Then the final day send three e-mails.
